1_answer_correctness.justification: Success is fully deterministic and verifiable in code: the server tracks each terminal's online flag and _get_status returns score=status['online']/max=status['total']; a run is 'done' only when all nested terminals are brought online via correct decoding/auth (e.g. Sys32Terminal._handle_authenticate checks the exact decoded token). No subjective judging.

2_construct_validity.justification: Implementation tightly isolates the claimed capability (cross-run learning + agentic mastery of nested terminals). Difficulty is staged via composed terminal specs (T1, T1-T1, ... up to T1|T2|T3|T4|T5), a persistent filesystem enforces the cross-run learning claim, and scoring blends success count with token/iteration efficiency as stated. Failures map to inability to solve the puzzle, not formatting confounds.

3_code_quality.justification: The dataset source is clean, modular, and well-commented: separate codecs, terminals, server, telemetry, sandboxed VirtualFileSystem with path-escape checks, robust tool dispatch with bare-exception-free error handling, retries, and dual GenAI/OpenAI tool loops. Uses kbench.task decorator idiomatically. Minor: the public notebook itself errored on import (LocalEnvironment AttributeError) in the captured run.

4_sample_size.justification: TaskSpecGenerator yields tasks_count=50 instances per generator across 10 staged generators (validation finds the easiest unsolved task across hundreds of generated terminal configurations), far exceeding 20 evaluation items.

5_metric_design.justification: Computes at least two distinct dimensions: success (online/total terminals) AND efficiency (total tokens and agentic iterations, with score = 100*passed_generators - fine where fine scales with iterations spent). RunTelemetry tracks token metrics and per-terminal activation steps. Multiple performance dimensions are explicitly combined.

6_problem_statement.justification: The task description and system.md name the target capability (mastering activation of unknown nested-terminal configurations as fast and token-efficiently as possible, with cross-run knowledge retention) and motivate it, but the public task page is thin and does not situate it against existing agentic benchmarks or articulate the specific gap filled.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Source code and prompts are heavily documented (each terminal has a .md spec, system prompt explains the protocol), but the published notebook has only a handful of brief markdown cells and no walkthrough of scoring, schema, or metric interpretation; reproduction relies on reading the dataset source. Partial coverage.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific, well-defined sub-capability (adaptive cross-run skill acquisition in agentic tool use) with a clear difficulty gradient (staged terminal compositions) and an explicit efficiency hypothesis. Controls include deterministic seeds across models and a persistent-folder mechanism that operationalizes the learning claim.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly organized into a structured difficulty taxonomy of 10 progressively harder generators (single terminal -> deep heterogeneous chains T1|T2|T3|T4|T5 repeated), and scoring reports how many generators were fully passed before failure, revealing a performance gradient and where models fail.

10_model_coverage.justification: The notebook runs against kbench.llm (a single target model) and the captured artifacts show one model; no multi-model cross-comparison is built into the task.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Combines deterministic guards (regex word-count 250-400, forbidden-token \bword s?\b checks) with a 4-judge 'senate' LLM rubric of 3-4 named criteria at a 75% pass threshold. The rubric criteria are binary yes/no questions, not anchored multi-level scales, and the lexical regex can miss morphological variants, so verification is partly fragile.

2_construct_validity.justification: Claims to measure 'epistemic exile / empathy over time' (explaining modern concepts within an old epistemic frame), and the lexical guillotine + period-framing criteria do target this, but the heavy mandatory formatting (brackets, scratchpad, strict word counts, language switching to Ge'ez/Spanish) conflates the target with instruction-following and formatting compliance, introducing moderate confounds.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional and readable with deterministic checks and a structured judge-voting ledger, but no markdown documentation, repeated copy-pasted boilerplate across 5 near-identical notebooks, brittle bracket-regex extraction, and silent early-return on a compromised senate (some notebooks just 'return' if judges != 4). Rough edges remain.

4_sample_size.justification: Each notebook is a single hand-crafted scenario (one llm.prompt call, one item). Even across all 5 notebooks there are only 5 distinct items; a single lucky/unlucky generation swings the result.

5_metric_design.justification: Multiple checks are computed (word-count, lexical purity, multi-criterion senate approval ratio) but they all measure the same underlying pass/fail dimension of a single response; no second performance axis (no calibration, latency, or graded scoring) and no documented mitigation of LLM-judge biases.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task names and descriptions clearly state the capability (e.g. 'Narrow AI vs AGI in Ge'ez for King Ezana', explaining modern phenomena without anachronistic vocabulary) with basic motivation, but no notebook markdown connects this to a broader research question or identifies the gap relative to existing evals.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Notebooks contain essentially no markdown documentation cells; the prompt, scoring rules, judge selection, and thresholds must be reverse-engineered from code. No dataset/schema description or scoring-methodology writeup.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a named domain (anachronism-constrained explanation / conceptual mapping) and shows some control via paired conditions (same AGI concept in Ge'ez vs English; same algorithm concept in English vs Spanish), but the design does not cleanly isolate the mechanism from formatting/instruction-following, and there is no aggregated cross-condition analysis in code.

9_item_diversity.justification: There is real variation across the 5 tasks (different eras, figures, concepts, and a deliberate language manipulation), giving surface diversity, but with one item per notebook there is no within-task taxonomy, difficulty stratification, or per-condition breakdown reported by the code.

10_model_coverage.justification: The benchmark evaluates a single target model via kbench.llm (the 4 'senate' models are judges, not evaluated targets); no cross-model comparison of the model under test.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is generated deterministically by the World-Swap engine (correct vs naive answers from apply_operator_swap, physics/linguistic/logic rule maps) and verified with normalized matching plus a multi-layer slop detector (keyword, numeric proximity, embedding similarity, token overlap) and a verbose-correct guard. Answers are unambiguous and fully verifiable.

2_construct_validity.justification: Strong isolation of metacognition: Tier-1 identity-rule problems serve as a scaffold-equalized control vs Tier-2+ counterfactual swaps (the prompts are matched except for the world rules, explicitly 'eliminating the scaffolding confound'), and the primary metric is SDT meta-d'/d' rather than raw accuracy, separating metacognitive efficiency from object-level ability.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular, heavily sectioned code: dataclasses, deterministic seeding, structured-output schema, parallel evaluation with kbench cache, _safe_call exception wrapping, protobuf sanitization, fallback embedding/token-overlap paths, and assertion-based pass/fail. Robust output parsing and edge handling throughout.

4_sample_size.justification: N=600 problems (150 per tier x 4 tiers, balanced across 4 problem types), vastly exceeding 20 and enabling stable per-tier and per-type SDT estimates with bootstrap CIs.

5_metric_design.justification: Computes many distinct dimensions: d', meta-d', M-ratio, ACE (asymmetric Brier), ECE, confidence entropy, selective-prediction score, tool-utility, four failure-mode rates, plus bootstrap 95% CIs. Markdown justifies why calibration/metacognition matter and the metric choices; confidence binning and asymmetric penalties are documented.

6_problem_statement.justification: The header markdown precisely names the target (metacognitive efficiency via SDT meta-d'/d'), enumerates four tasks and four failure modes, and explains the diagnostic intent and why decision-theoretic scoring beats plain accuracy, situating the capability gap clearly.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Comprehensive: numbered markdown sections (World-Swap engine, slop detection, dataset generator, evaluation, prompts, aggregator), inline docstrings explaining each metric and the scaffolding-confound fix, dataset construction (tiers/types/counts) documented, and metric interpretation given. Reproducible from the notebook alone.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (inhibitory metacognitive monitoring under counterfactual world-swaps) with explicit experimental controls (identity-rule Tier 1 baseline, difficulty gradient Tiers 1-4, congruent-vs-swap design) and clear hypotheses (inverse scaling index, inhibitory accuracy, semantic slop as prior-leakage).

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized into an explicit taxonomy of 4 difficulty tiers x 4 problem types, and the aggregator reports accuracy and confidence broken down per tier (Tier1Acc..Tier4Acc, tier confs) and per failure mode, exposing exactly where models fail.

10_model_coverage.justification: Run against a single target model (kbench.llm; captured artifact is claude-opus-4-7); no built-in multi-model comparison.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth (answer/correct_answer/naive_answer) exists in the large dataset and the task-2 ambiguity notebook checks it via substring match, but verification is fragile (case/substring only) and seriously broken elsewhere: the main eval scored 0/734 on v4 OverlapQuery, and the v2/v4 notebooks assert only that an internal Python store returns non-None, never comparing to the question's true answer.

2_construct_validity.justification: Most notebooks do not measure LLM temporal reasoning at all: the v2/v4 tasks call store.get() on a hand-written TemporalAttentionStore and assert it returns a value while never using llm, so 'CurrentQuery: 942/942 correct' reflects the Python data structure, not the model. The staleness notebook uses a 3-item MockStore. There is a clear mismatch between stated purpose and what runs.

3_code_quality.justification: Code runs and has reasonable helpers (normalize_fact, replay_events, get_question_day), but bare 'except: pass' swallows all errors in the eval loops, the main notebook hardcodes fabricated 'key_findings', tasks accept llm yet ignore it, and one notebook depends on MockStore mocks. Functional but with significant rough edges and dead/misleading logic.

4_sample_size.justification: The dataset is large (2000 questions per version/seed, multiple versions v1-v4 x seeds), and the task-2 ambiguity notebook evaluates over all 2000 rows; well above 20 items.

5_metric_design.justification: A single accuracy/pass-fail metric per task with minimal justification; the store-based notebooks effectively compute only data-presence rate, and the fabricated multi-line 'key_findings' (TRS, correlations, p-values) are hardcoded constants not derived from any computation, not a real metric suite.

6_problem_statement.justification: The eval notebook header names the capability (temporal reasoning, validity windows vs decay) with basic motivation and a competition/track, but does not rigorously articulate the gap or research question beyond slogans, and the description is undercut by tasks that do not test it.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Some documentation exists (dataset path/structure described, task families listed, a few markdown headers and the data-loading print of question keys), but scoring rules, the meaning of facts/events schemas, and how ground truth is derived are underspecified, and several notebooks have no markdown at all.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets the right domain (point-in-time / as-of temporal QA with traps for stale-vs-current and overlapping validity windows) and defines task families as a coarse difficulty/condition split, but lacks working experimental controls because the harness largely fails to attribute performance to the model (no LLM in most tasks; broken scoring).

9_item_diversity.justification: Items carry task_family labels (AsOfQA, ChangeDetection, CausalQuery, PastQueryTrap, OverlapQuery, DecayTrap, CurrentQuery) and version tiers v1-v4 giving structured variation, but the notebooks do not report a clean per-condition gradient of model performance (results are 0/734 or measure the store), so the diversity is not turned into a usable performance breakdown.

10_model_coverage.justification: Across the collection different notebooks reference different models (google/gemini-2.5-flash, zai/glm-5, anthropic claude-opus in the run artifacts), giving 2-3 distinct models, though no single task systematically compares them.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Fully deterministic verification: Code Golf execs each submitted option against pre-validated hidden assert tests in a fresh namespace (pass/fail + exact char count); Regex Golf compiles patterns and checks re.fullmatch over MATCH/DO_NOT_MATCH lists; EGAT computes an exact geometric center-of-mass distance to a fixed centroid. No subjective judging.

2_construct_validity.justification: Implementation closely matches the 'constraint adaptation' claim: each sub-benchmark forces the model to optimize under an unusual hard constraint (shortest correct code, shortest valid regex, move token CoM toward a centroid) with a paired baseline so the score is a fractional improvement over a fixed reference, isolating adaptation/optimization rather than raw knowledge.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, well-commented, modular: regex-based option extraction, sandboxed exec with limited traceback, multi-trial reprompting with feedback, graceful API-error retry/backoff, system-role-rejection fallback to baked prompts, deterministic dedup ordering, and defensive re-imports. Idiomatic kbench.task usage with assertions.

4_sample_size.justification: Each sub-benchmark uses 5 tasks/prompts/sessions (5 code-golf tasks, 5 regex sessions, 5 EGAT prompts). Each is run over 10 trials, but the number of distinct evaluation items per task is 5 (10-20 range only if trials are counted), placing it at the lower-mid sample tier.

5_metric_design.justification: Beyond pass/fail, the metric is a continuous fractional-improvement score against a fixed baseline (char reduction, or CoM distance reduction D_initial->D_final), aggregated as a mean with per-item reporting and best-of-trials tracking; the markdown justifies the scoring and its sign conventions, and EGAT documents tokenizer/normalization choices to avoid model-family bias.

6_problem_statement.justification: Each notebook's markdown clearly names its capability (embedding-geometry awareness, code compression, regex/pattern compression) and the scoring rationale, but does not tie them to a broader research question or explain the gap relative to existing benchmarks; motivation is present but not compelling/specific about novelty.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Strong notebook documentation: markdown explains scoring formulas, configuration flags, and per-task tables (baseline chars/length), and code comments thoroughly describe design choices (e.g. whitespace-token filtering rationale, set()-dedup determinism, system-role fallback). Schemas of the in-notebook datasets are explicit.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes specific, well-defined sub-capabilities (optimization under a precise hard constraint) with a clear hypothesis and built-in controls: a fixed validated baseline per item, difficulty tiers (e.g. EGAT prompt_001 single token up to prompt_005 19 adversarial tokens; regex baselines 21->56 chars), and best-across-trials tracking that exposes an adaptation gradient.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly structured into labeled difficulty tiers with per-item baselines and the code reports per-item scores (per-prompt/per-task lists) alongside the aggregate, enabling analysis of where models succeed (e.g. EGAT scores 0.33 on easy single-token vs near-0 on adversarial mixed-token prompts).

10_model_coverage.justification: Each sub-benchmark runs against a single target model via kbench.llm; the captured run shows one model and no cross-model comparison is built in.
